story_title: Silver Thursday — The Hunt Brothers' Attempt to Corner the Silver Market
summary: Between 1979 and 1980, Texas oil billionaires Nelson Bunker Hunt and William Herbert Hunt accumulated an estimated 100 million troy ounces of silver — roughly one-third of the world's privately held supply — driving the price from $6 per ounce to nearly $50 before a rule change on COMEX triggered a collapse that destroyed their fortune and nearly brought down a major brokerage.
recommended_arc: Hook: two Texas billionaires decide fiat currency is doomed — they'll corner silver instead. Rising: silver rockets from $6 to nearly $50 an ounce; the Hunts control a third of the world's supply. Climax: COMEX changes margin rules overnight; Silver Thursday, 27 March 1980. Payoff: the brothers lose $1.7 billion in a day; their $1 billion bailout bail-out reshapes derivatives regulation.

verified_facts.claim: Nelson Bunker Hunt and William Herbert Hunt, sons of Texas oil billionaire H.L. Hunt, began systematically accumulating physical silver and futures contracts in 1973 as a hedge against inflation and dollar devaluation; their buying accelerated dramatically in late 1979.

At the peak of their position in early 1980, the Hunt brothers and their associates controlled an estimated 100 million troy ounces of silver — roughly one-third of the world's total privately held silver supply.
Silver prices rose from approximately $6 per troy ounce in early 1979 to a record high of $49.45 on 18 January 1980, an increase of over 700 percent in 12 months, partly driven by the Hunts' buying.

On 7 January 1980, the Chicago Board of Trade (CBOT) and COMEX implemented 'Silver Rule 7,' limiting new silver futures positions to 3 million ounces and requiring holders of existing long positions to reduce their holdings, effectively halting the Hunts' ability to accumulate further.

On 27 March 1980 — known as 'Silver Thursday' — silver prices collapsed by approximately 50 percent in a single day, falling from around $21.62 to $10.80 per ounce, triggered by the Hunts' inability to meet margin calls.

The Hunt brothers' failure to meet margin calls threatened to bankrupt the brokerage firm Bache Halsey Stuart Shields; a consortium of major US banks arranged a $1.1 billion bailout loan to the Hunts in March 1980 to prevent broader market contagion.

In 1988 the Hunt brothers pleaded no contest to civil charges of market manipulation brought by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and were fined $10 million; Nelson Bunker Hunt filed for personal bankruptcy in 1988, listing debts of approximately $2.5 billion.
